pairs.definition: A cell with one copy of each chromosome (n = 23 in humans). Gametes are haploid.
A cell with two copies of each chromosome (2n = 46 in humans). Most body cells.
Exchange of DNA between non-sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes during prophase I.
Failure of chromosomes to separate properly in anaphase, producing aneuploid gametes.
Random orientation of homologous pairs at metaphase I, mixing maternal and paternal alleles.
Visible X-shaped sites where non-sister chromatids physically exchange DNA segments.
An individual's two alleles for a given gene (e.g. YY, Yy, yy).
The observable characteristic resulting from a genotype (e.g. yellow vs. green seed).
Carrying one dominant and one recessive allele (e.g. Yy). Carriers are heterozygous.
Both alleles are fully expressed in the heterozygote — both phenotypes visible (ABO blood AB).
One gene with multiple, seemingly unrelated phenotypic effects (e.g. Marfan syndrome).
A trait controlled by multiple genes, producing continuous variation (e.g. skin color).
Unwinds the DNA double helix at the replication fork by breaking hydrogen bonds.
Synthesizes a short RNA primer to give DNA polymerase a free 3'-OH to extend.
Main replication enzyme — adds nucleotides 5'→3' and proofreads mismatches.
Replaces RNA primers with DNA nucleotides on the lagging strand.
Seals the gaps between Okazaki fragments by forming the final phosphodiester bond.
Extends the 3' ends of telomeres using its own RNA template; active in germ/stem/cancer cells.
Synthesis of RNA from a DNA template by RNA polymerase, in the nucleus.
Synthesis of a protein from an mRNA template at a ribosome, in the cytoplasm.
A 3-nucleotide sequence on mRNA specifying one amino acid (or a stop signal).
The 3-nucleotide sequence on tRNA that pairs with a complementary mRNA codon.
Non-coding sequence within a gene, transcribed into pre-mRNA but spliced out before translation.
The expressed portion of a gene, retained in mature mRNA and translated into protein.
Amplifies a specific DNA sequence exponentially via cycles of denaturation, annealing, and extension.
Separates DNA fragments by size — smaller fragments travel farther through agarose under voltage.
Short tandem repeat — a short DNA sequence repeated many times; repeat count varies between individuals.
DNA composed of segments from different sources joined together (e.g. human insulin gene in a plasmid).
Short single-stranded DNA that defines where PCR amplification starts and provides a 3'-OH for the polymerase.
Heat-stable DNA polymerase from a thermophilic bacterium; survives the 95°C denaturation step of PCR.
Two identical copies of one chromosome (joined at centromere) made during DNA replication.
Matching pair of chromosomes — same genes in same order, possibly different alleles, one from each parent.
Continuously synthesized DNA strand built toward the replication fork in the 5'→3' direction.
Discontinuously synthesized DNA built away from the fork as Okazaki fragments.
%A = %T and %G = %C in any double-stranded DNA, due to complementary base pairing.
Genetic information flows: DNA → RNA → Protein, via transcription and translation.
